title: Fix WebSocket reconnect/resume state loss
description:
## PROBLEM
Random disconnect/reconnect events sometimes make dashboard data vanish. Audit points to `apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/lib/ws.ts`: `unsubscribe()` deletes `lastSeqByChannel`, weakening reconnect resume. `github:sync_hint` also calls `load()`, which can transiently reset/replace store data instead of preserving stale visible state.

## SUCCESS
WebSocket reconnect preserves channel sequence where safe, resumes without data loss, and sync hints trigger merge/refresh without clearing visible GitHub/beads state. Data remains visible through disconnect/reconnect.

## SCOPE
- apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/lib/ws.ts
- apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/hooks/useWebSocket.ts
- apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/hooks/useGithubActivity.ts
- relevant websocket/store tests

## VALIDATION
- Test unsubscribe/remount does not clear last sequence for reconnect resume.
- Test github:sync_hint does not clear existing store lists while refresh pending/failing.
- Test disconnect/reconnect keeps visible GitHub PR/issues rows.
notes:
Patched WsClient unsubscribe preserve last seq; sync_hint now background refreshes visible state; added resume/preserve-state tests.

title: Make GitHub tabs high-availability and warm from local DB
description:
## PROBLEM
GitHub DB is populated and list routes are DB-first, but PR->Issues and first Releases tab can still feel slow because UI fetch orchestration is cold/gated. Data should remain visible immediately from store/cache/DB; refresh should happen in background.

## SUCCESS
PRs/issues/releases render last-known cached/store data immediately, warm likely tabs after repo selection, and never show an empty/loading-only pane when usable cached data exists. Stale refreshes merge and never erase newer WS rows.

## SCOPE
- apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/components/shell/MainPane.tsx
- apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/hooks/useGithubActivity.ts
- apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/stores/github.ts
- apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/lib/client.ts
- apps/gitboard/src/api/routes/github.ts only for freshness/source metadata if needed
- tests for MainPane/useGithubActivity/client

## VALIDATION
- PR->Issues tab switch renders cached DB/store rows without waiting for slow fetch.
- First Releases switch shows cached/stale rows or fast empty-cache metadata, not multi-second blank loading.
- Slow/failing tab fetch does not clear visible rows.
- WS upsert remains authoritative over HTTP responses.
notes:
High-availability cache path now preserves visible GitHub rows on empty/slow refreshes, warms tab state from store+cache immediately, and adds regression tests for store clear semantics, sync_hint refresh, and cached tab rendering.

title: Design high-availability dashboard data plane
description:
## PROBLEM

Gitboard still has multi-second perceived latency switching GitHub tabs and console surfaces even though GitHub DB data exists locally. The dashboard needs a high-availability data plane: always serve visible cached/local state fast, keep WebSocket realtime authoritative, and move expensive data gathering out of UI request paths.

## SUCCESS

Produce a verified architecture and implementation plan for separating dashboard UI/API reads from data ingestion/refresh. Decide whether in-process cache, SQLite materialized views, background workers, Redis, or another queue/cache is appropriate. Identify current blocking request paths and propose staged fixes with validation.

## SCOPE

- GitHub data flow: apps/gitboard/src/api/routes/github.ts, core/github-store.ts, core/github-poller.ts, dashboard hooks/stores/MainPane.
- Specialists data flow: api/routes/specialists.ts, server/observability/*, specialist DB attach pool.
- Beads/graph data flow enough to explain head-of-line blocking: api/routes/beads.ts, api/routes/graph.ts, core/graph-dao.ts, dashboard hooks.
- Runtime/container deployment data paths and DB location.

## NON_GOALS

- No implementation edits in this bead unless follow-up implementation issue is created/claimed.
- No broad rewrite without phased migration plan.

## CONSTRAINTS

- WebSocket updates remain instant and authoritative.
- Dashboard API should serve stale/local data immediately when available.
- GitHub REST and specialist DB scans must not block tab/surface navigation.
- Prefer simplest reliable design; Redis only if justified over SQLite/in-process cache/background jobs.

## VALIDATION

- Map current slow paths with code evidence and log evidence.
- Verify whether /api/github/issues itself is slow or whether UI/server is head-of-line blocked.
- Produce instrumentation plan: server route timings + browser tab click/fetch/paint timings.

## OUTPUT

Architecture recommendation, phased implementation plan, risks, and verification checklist.
notes: External verification summary received: DB available and GitHub list routes DB-first; PR/issues latency likely frontend orchestration; releases cold no preload; websocket unsubscribe deletes lastSeqByChannel causing weak resume/sync_hint reload; graph/specialists/beads still block Bun event loop via sync Dolt/attach work. Recommendation: no Redis for now; single-node async Dolt + in-process SWR.
close_reason: Epic complete: all 4 planned children (forge-ma82, forge-pula, forge-5i43, forge-pbev) shipped + 7 discovered-from siblings (forge-9qmk, forge-vdda, forge-ia5u, forge-jve5, forge-ijzx, forge-cspw, forge-g598) shipped. HA layer (DB = availability, WS = freshness, REST = replenishment) structurally sound. Cold parallel <50ms, warm graph <300ms. Session report: .xtrm/reports/2026-05-21-42192f57.md

title: Replace pipe shell fallback with real PTY terminal provider
description: PROBLEM: Current fallback shell proves websocket transport but behaves poorly: no real PTY semantics, job-control warnings, weak interactive behavior. Wetty demonstrates the right architecture: real PTY + xterm + websocket. SUCCESS: Local provider uses a real PTY implementation when shell provider is explicitly enabled; falls back to disabled state if PTY dependency is unavailable; cwd/shell/env/rate-limit/security gates remain enforced. SCOPE: provider-registry/local PTY integration, dependency decision, graceful unavailable status, tests. NON_GOALS: SSH/Tailscale/tmux, multi-user auth beyond current gates. VALIDATION: terminal can run pwd/echo/interactive input without job-control warnings; provider tests pass; security tests retained.
close_reason: Done: real terminal now uses Node helper-owned node-pty with cwd/shell policy, env allowlist, origin gating, and focused validation.

title: Harden TerminalStream xterm lifecycle, focus, and resize
description: PROBLEM: Terminal UI throws xterm renderer dimension errors and input can stop working due React rerenders/remount/focus/fit races. SUCCESS: xterm is created once per mount, callbacks stay fresh without remount, focus works on click/open, fit is deferred until visible, drawer close/open does not trigger renderer exceptions. SCOPE: TerminalStream lifecycle, FitAddon timing, ResizeObserver, tests for input/focus/remount/zero-size behavior. NON_GOALS: backend/provider changes. VALIDATION: no browser console xterm dimension errors; focused TerminalStream/TerminalTabPanel tests pass; manual typing works.
close_reason: Done: TerminalStream lifecycle stabilized, font metrics hardened, resize/focus guarded, tests updated.

title: Stabilize Gitboard dev server and websocket proxy
description: PROBLEM: Browser can hit stale/wrong backend/frontend processes and Vite did not proxy /api/console/terminal/ws as websocket, causing disconnected terminal and project load confusion. SUCCESS: One documented dev startup path runs backend and dashboard from current worktree; /api/github/repos loads via Vite; /api/console/terminal/status works; /api/console/terminal/ws upgrades through Vite; no stale IPv6/old-main process confusion. SCOPE: vite.config proxy, dev scripts/docs or guards, startup verification commands. VALIDATION: HTTP/API/WS smoke checks from frontend port. NON_GOALS: production deployment redesign.
close_reason: Done: dev server stabilized with explicit terminal websocket proxy and IPv4 backend binding.

title: Terminal UX recovery and Wetty-style PTY hardening
description: PROBLEM: Post-merge terminal support is functionally unstable and visually poor. User reports project loading regressions, disconnected/invalid-message states, xterm renderer crashes, and no usable input. code-server is a broad UX benchmark; Wetty is the direct terminal-over-web reference. SUCCESS: Gitboard has a reliable, good-looking terminal drawer using a real PTY-style backend, stable websocket/protocol path, robust xterm lifecycle/focus/fit behavior, and reproducible manual/automated smoke validation. SCOPE: Decompose and fix terminal/runtime regressions from current xt/yz1j work. Cover dev server/proxy consistency, provider architecture, xterm UI/lifecycle, security invariants, and smoke tests. NON_GOALS: No full VS Code clone, no SSH/Tailscale/tmux unless explicitly planned later, no broad app redesign. VALIDATION: project APIs load, terminal connects, accepts input, shows output, survives drawer close/reopen, no xterm renderer crashes, focused tests and manual smoke pass.
close_reason: Done: terminal recovery children for runtime, PTY, lifecycle, UI polish, and regression coverage are complete.

title: Fix post-merge Gitboard loading and terminal UI regressions
description: PROBLEM: After terminal E2E merge, user reports projects do not load and Terminal UI looks wrong/broken on dev server. SUCCESS: Gitboard loads project data again and Terminal tab styling/functionality is consistent with app design. SCOPE: Diagnose current xt/yz1j server/client errors, project-loading APIs/UI, TerminalTabPanel/TerminalStream styling/wiring as needed. NON_GOALS: no broad redesign, no unrelated backend refactor. CONSTRAINTS: Manual orchestrator intervention authorized by user; still keep changes minimal and run focused validation. VALIDATION: dev server logs clean of relevant errors; project endpoints/load path works; terminal visual/functionality smoke; focused tests. OUTPUT: files changed, errors found/fixed, validation.
close_reason: Done: post-merge loading/runtime regressions fixed including IPv4 backend binding, websocket proxy, protocol envelopes, and terminal runtime smoke.

title: Final security review terminal E2E
description: PROBLEM: Prior security review forge-qdtv failed only because attach used payload.token instead of payload.reattachToken. Debugger forge-nh4e fixed it on feature/forge-lqyc.6-executor. Need final mandatory security review before merge. SUCCESS: PASS if attach payload now requires reattachToken; legacy token rejected; missing reattachToken rejected; bridge/UI/tests use reattachToken; original TERM-001/TERM-002 remain fixed: server-minted unguessable token distinct from sessionId; foreign websocket cannot attach/input/resize/exit with sessionId alone; session/token/output not persisted in localStorage; valid reconnect with token works. SCOPE: current feature/forge-lqyc.6-executor after job 6c4f9a. Files: packages/core/src/terminal/protocol.ts, packages/core/tests/terminal-protocol.test.ts, api/terminal/bridge.ts, TerminalTabPanel.tsx, shell store, terminal route/store/panel tests. VALIDATION EVIDENCE: protocol+route tests pass 12/12; UI panel needs DOM env in this harness. OUTPUT: Security verdict PASS/PARTIAL/FAIL and merge readiness.
notes:
Review complete. Verified protocol requires payload.reattachToken; payload.token rejected; missing token rejected; bridge mints random reattachToken distinct from sessionId; foreign connection blocked from input/resize/exit without ownership; shell store keeps terminal session/token/output in memory only; UI reattach path and tests use reattachToken. Used local code evidence plus orchestrator test pass artifact (19 tests / 4 files).
### Specialist Output ŌĆö security-auditor (job 1d65e0) [WAITING ŌĆö more output may follow]

## Summary
Scoped terminal security review PASS. Merge ready.

## Status
Final verdict: **PASS**  
Overall risk: **low**  
Scope reviewed:
- `packages/core/src/terminal/protocol.ts`
- `packages/core/tests/terminal-protocol.test.ts`
- `apps/gitboard/src/api/terminal/bridge.ts`
- `apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/components/shell/TerminalTabPanel.tsx`
- `apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/stores/shell.ts`
- `apps/gitboard/tests/api/routes/terminal.test.ts`
- `apps/gitboard/tests/dashboard/stores/shell.test.ts`
- `apps/gitboard/tests/dashboard/components/shell/TerminalTabPanel.test.tsx`
- `apps/gitboard/tests/dashboard/components/terminal/TerminalStream.test.tsx`

Modes used:
- static secure-code review
- bounty-style exploitability triage

## Changes
No file changes. Scan-only review.

## Verification
- `packages/core/src/terminal/protocol.ts:223-225` attach validation requires `payload.reattachToken` non-empty string.
- `packages/core/tests/terminal-protocol.test.ts:24-26` valid `reattachToken` accepted; missing token rejected; legacy `payload.token` rejected.
- `apps/gitboard/src/api/terminal/bridge.ts:94` server mints `crypto.randomUUID()` reattach token.
- `apps/gitboard/src/api/terminal/bridge.ts:113-125` attach path checks exact `reattachToken`; invalid or missing token gets `forbidden`.
- `apps/gitboard/src/api/terminal/bridge.ts:141-170` foreign socket blocked from `input`/`resize`/`exit` unless owner connection.
- `apps/gitboard/tests/api/routes/terminal.test.ts:122-140` foreign connection control denied with 3 `forbidden` errors.
- `apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/components/shell/TerminalTabPanel.tsx:37-39` UI sends `payload.reattachToken` on attach.
- `apps/gitboard/tests/dashboard/components/shell/TerminalTabPanel.test.tsx:81-82` remount/reattach test asserts `payload.reattachToken === "token-1"`.
- `apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/stores/shell.ts:16-23,55-57,159-169` terminal session id, reattach token, output kept in memory only; not persisted to `localStorage`.
- `apps/gitboard/tests/dashboard/stores/shell.test.ts:20-35` rehydrate test proves terminal session/output not restored from storage.
- Orchestrator validation artifact: `apps/gitboard/tests/api/routes/terminal.test.ts`, `packages/core/tests/terminal-protocol.test.ts`, `apps/gitboard/tests/dashboard/stores/shell.test.ts`, `apps/gitboard/tests/dashboard/components/terminal/TerminalStream.test.tsx` passed; reported 19/19 tests passed. User supplied extra DOM-env note removes prior harness false negative.

## Risks
- No reachable auth/session bypass found in scoped terminal flow.
- Residual low risk: reattach token remains bearer token in client memory until exit/remount cleanup. Acceptable in current scoped model; not persisted.

## Summary

## Security Audit Summary
- Scope reviewed: `apps/gitboard/src/api/terminal/bridge.ts`, `apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/components/shell/TerminalTabPanel.tsx`, `apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/stores/shell.ts`, `apps/gitboard/tests/api/routes/terminal.test.ts`, `apps/gitboard/tests/dashboard/components/shell/TerminalTabPanel.test.tsx`, `apps/gitboard/tests/dashboard/stores/shell.test.ts`

  - local source evidence only

Terminal wiring fail security review. Main break: session ownership removed, replaced with bearer-style `sessionId`/`streamId` control. Persisted `sessionId` in `localStorage` makes reuse easier. Merge blocked pending fixes.

## Status

- Verdict: FAIL
- Merge readiness: not ready

## Findings

- ID: TERM-001
- Severity: high
- Category/CWE when applicable: CWE-384 Session Fixation / CWE-639 Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key
- Evidence:
  - `apps/gitboard/src/api/terminal/bridge.ts:103-155` ŌĆö `attach`, `input`, `resize`, `exit` no longer check connection ownership; only `sessionId` + `streamId`
  - `apps/gitboard/src/api/terminal/bridge.ts` diff vs `xt/yz1j` removes `ownerConnectionId` gate
  - `apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/components/shell/TerminalTabPanel.tsx:34-43,84-91,103` ŌĆö client uses same value for `streamId` and `sessionId`
  - `apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/stores/shell.ts:55-56,155-165` ŌĆö `terminalSessionId` persisted in `localStorage`
  - `apps/gitboard/tests/api/routes/terminal.test.ts:67-86` ŌĆö test changed from deny cross-connection control to allow reattach/control from new connection
- Reachability/exploitability:
  - same-origin tab/script can read persisted `terminalSessionId`
  - same-origin tab/script can open websocket, send `attach`, then `input`/`resize`/`exit`
  - no server-side bind to original websocket or user-specific secret in scoped code
- Impact:
  - PTY session hijack
  - command injection into already-open shell
  - forced resize/exit
  - cross-tab misuse exactly within task threat model
- Recommended fix:
  - restore server-side ownership proof for mutating actions
  - if reattach needed, use server-minted unguessable reattach token separate from `sessionId`
  - bind reattach token to authenticated user/session and expire on detach/timeout
  - keep `streamId` distinct from authorization secret
  - add negative tests: foreign connection cannot control session without valid reattach credential
- Verification after fix:
  - test that second websocket without reattach token gets `forbidden`
  - test that stolen `sessionId` from `localStorage` alone cannot `attach`/`input`/`exit`
  - test legit reconnect with valid server-issued token still works

- ID: TERM-002
- Severity: medium
- Category/CWE when applicable: CWE-922 Insecure Storage of Sensitive Information / CWE-200 Exposure of Sensitive Information
- Evidence:
  - `apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/stores/shell.ts:55-56` ŌĆö rehydrates `terminalSessionId` and `terminalOutput` from `localStorage`
  - `apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/stores/shell.ts:157,164,170` ŌĆö writes live session identifier and terminal output to `localStorage`
  - `apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/components/shell/TerminalTabPanel.tsx:55-57` ŌĆö all output appended for persistence
- Reachability/exploitability:
  - terminal output often contains repo paths, logs, tokens, command results
  - data survives tab close/reopen and browser restarts
  - same-origin script, shared workstation user, browser extension with storage access can recover data
  - persisted `terminalSessionId` extends TERM-001 hijack window during 30s cleanup
- Impact:
  - sensitive terminal transcript at rest in browser
  - easier live-session reattach after tab close/remount
- Recommended fix:
  - do not persist live `terminalSessionId` in `localStorage`
  - avoid persisting terminal output by default; keep in memory or use explicit opt-in encrypted/session-scoped cache
  - clear output/session immediately on detach, drawer close, logout, provider exit
  - if persistence required, redact/size-limit by bytes and use `sessionStorage` minimum
- Verification after fix:
  - inspect browser storage after terminal use; no live session credential persisted
  - confirm terminal transcript absent after reload unless explicit secure opt-in
  - test drawer remount path using secure reattach token flow, not `localStorage` session reuse

## Non-findings / Dropped Noise

- No direct command injection sink found in reviewed client code. Input forwarded to terminal provider by design; main issue is who can send input, not string interpolation in scoped code.
- No path traversal found in scoped files.

title: Attack plan: implement terminal stream substrate safely
description:
PROBLEM: The embedded terminal epic forge-lqyc is ready for implementation, but a fresh orchestrator session needs a clear, safe execution path. Raw shell exposure is the highest-risk surface, so implementation must proceed in the right order with specialist review gates.
SUCCESS: Fresh orchestrator can pick this bead up, dispatch the right specialist chain in order, and avoid starting PTY/WebSocket shell work before protocol and safety contracts exist.
SCOPE: Orchestration plan for epic forge-lqyc and child beads forge-lqyc.1 through forge-lqyc.11. This is a coordination/attack-plan bead, not an implementation bead.
NON_GOALS: Do not implement code in this bead. Do not dispatch PTY work first. Do not make tmux mandatory. Do not implement SSH/Tailscale. Do not fold specialist-feed reuse into the PTY MVP.
CONSTRAINTS: Use specialists-v3 workflow. Run specialists list --full before dispatch. Re-read target bead before each chain. Use executor -> reviewer for implementation chains. Run security-auditor before any PTY/shell-capable code lands. Keep TerminalStream renderer separate from provider/session ownership. Treat shell exposure as high-risk.

ATTACK PLAN:

1. Start with protocol contract.
   - Target bead: forge-lqyc.2 Define terminal stream protocol.
   - Dispatch executor on forge-lqyc.2.
   - Scope should include shared TypeScript protocol/types/schema/tests only.
   - Must define versioned generic envelope + terminal-specific payloads, not broad RPC.
   - Must include session/stream ids, capabilities, states, error model, resize/input/output semantics, and backpressure/byte-volume notes.
   - Review with reviewer. Merge only after PASS.

2. In parallel or next, build renderer-only component.
   - Target bead: forge-lqyc.1 Create reusable TerminalStream xterm component.
   - Dispatch executor on forge-lqyc.1 once file overlap with protocol is understood.
   - Must not import provider/session/PTY APIs.
   - Must support readonly and read/write modes, ANSI output, scrollback, fit addon, resize, lifecycle cleanup.
   - Review with reviewer. Merge only after PASS.

3. Implement safety gates before PTY.
   - Target bead: forge-lqyc.7 Add shell-provider safety gates.
   - Depends on protocol.
   - Dispatch executor, then mandatory security-auditor on the executor job, then reviewer.
   - Safety must include: production disabled by default, explicit enable flag, localhost/admin intent, cwd allowlist, shell allowlist, env scrub, session caps, idle timeout, hard TTL, input/output byte limits, create/terminate audit events only, no command/input/output/secret logging, orphan cleanup, readonly feed permission separated from shell permission.
   - Do not start forge-lqyc.3 or forge-lqyc.4 before this lands.

4. Implement local PTY provider.
   - Target bead: forge-lqyc.3 Implement local PTY provider.
   - Depends on forge-lqyc.2 and forge-lqyc.7.
   - Dispatch executor after safety PASS/merge.
   - Use node-pty or vetted equivalent.
   - Provider owns process lifecycle; UI must not know PTY details.
   - Run security-auditor because this touches shell/process/input handling.
   - Review with reviewer. Merge only after PASS.

5. Add WebSocket bridge.
   - Target bead: forge-lqyc.4 Add terminal WebSocket bridge.
   - Depends on protocol, safety, PTY provider.
   - Dispatch executor after PTY provider shape is stable.
   - Must safely reject invalid session IDs and disabled providers; detach/schedule cleanup on disconnect; avoid logging user input.
   - Run security-auditor because browser-to-shell transport is sensitive.
   - Review with reviewer. Merge only after PASS.

6. Add drawer tab and end-to-end wiring.
   - Target beads: forge-lqyc.5 Add Terminal tab to bottom drawer, then forge-lqyc.6 Wire local terminal end-to-end.
   - Drawer tab can start after renderer if protocol overlap is low, but e2e wiring waits for websocket/PT provider.
   - Validate open/type/output/resize/close/reopen/exit/error/build.
   - Review with reviewer.

7. Tests.
   - Target bead: forge-lqyc.11 Terminal stream test coverage.
   - Can be split or run after substrate is stable.
   - Must cover TerminalStream mocks, resize, readonly blocking, protocol validation, PTY lifecycle, websocket bridge, safety gates, specialist-feed provider if implemented.

8. Follow-ups, not MVP blockers.
   - forge-lqyc.8 readonly specialist feed provider is now P3 follow-up after local PTY MVP proves renderer/protocol.
   - forge-lqyc.9 optional tmux evaluation after PTY stable.
   - forge-lqyc.10 SSH/Tailscale planning only; no SSH implementation.

KNOWN DESIGN DECISION FROM OVERTHINKER:
- Recommendation was ADJUST, applied.
- TerminalStream owns rendering only.
- TerminalSessionProvider owns PTY/tmux/SSH lifecycle.
- Protocol uses generic envelope + terminal event payloads.
- Local PTY provider only in MVP.
- tmux optional follow-up.
- SSH/Tailscale future provider only.
- Specialist-feed reuse follow-up, not MVP blocker.

VALIDATION FOR THIS ATTACK PLAN:
- bd show forge-lqyc and children before dispatch.
- sp ps and git worktree list before/after each dispatch.
- Reviewers must PASS before merge.
- security-auditor must run before shell-capable PTY/WebSocket changes merge.
- gitnexus impact/detect_changes requirements still apply for code edits.

OUTPUT: A future orchestrator should report which chain is dispatched first, job ids, review verdicts, merge status, and any deviations from this plan.
acceptance_criteria: Fresh orchestrator can follow the ordered plan without extra context; protocol and renderer are identified as safe first work; safety gates are explicitly required before PTY/WebSocket shell work; mandatory security-auditor gates are named for shell/process/browser-to-shell surfaces; tmux, SSH/Tailscale, and specialist-feed are clearly marked non-MVP/follow-up; plan references exact child bead ids; no implementation is done in this bead.

title: Drawer height ignored by flex:1 on .ide-main children ŌĆö opt drawer out
description:
## PROBLEM
globals.css line 4387: `.ide-main > * { flex: 1; min-height: 0; min-width: 0; }` applies `flex: 1` to ALL direct children of .ide-main, including the new `<BottomDrawer>`. flex:1 (shorthand for `flex: 1 1 0%`) overrides any `style={{ height: <px> }}` set inline. Result: BottomDrawer's height-driven resize / maximize / 75vh-default all UPDATE the store + inline style correctly but the RENDERED visual height never changes ŌĆö flex distributes available space proportionally instead.

Operator-confirmed via Firefox DevTools console: move/maximize handlers fire with correct math (h=106..139 as expected, maximize toggle logs show new heights) but the drawer doesn't visually resize.

Playwright tests missed this because we only checked `element.style.height` (the inline style we wrote), never `getBoundingClientRect().height` (what flex actually renders).

## SUCCESS
- Visual drawer height matches the value set via store (drag changes visible height in real time, maximize jumps, 75vh default opens at ~75vh).
- `getBoundingClientRect().height` Ōēł store drawerHeight ┬▒2px (the ┬▒2 accounts for sub-pixel rounding).
- Also: simultaneously fix sibling `.ide-main-content` to claim the remaining flex space (flex:1) so MainPane content stays scrollable. Without this the content area would have undefined height once drawer opts out.

## SCOPE
- apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/styles/globals.css
  - Add after line 4391: `.ide-main > .bottom-drawer { flex: 0 0 auto; }` ŌĆö drawer claims explicit pixel height only.
  - Keep `.ide-main > * { flex: 1; ... }` for `.ide-main-content` so it expands.
- apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/components/shell/BottomDrawer.tsx
  - REMOVE the three console.log diagnostic lines (operator confirmed module load works; logs are noise now).

## NON_GOALS
- No changes to drawer JSX, no changes to resize handler, no changes to store.

## CONSTRAINTS
- HARD: no gitnexus_* calls.

## VALIDATION
- bunx tsc -p apps/gitboard --noEmit clean.
- Playwright sanity: open drawer, getBoundingClientRect().height should equal the store drawerHeight value. Then drag, recheck ŌĆö rect.height changes. Maximize ŌĆö rect.height = viewport - 24.

## OUTPUT
- 2-file diff
- Confirmation that .bottom-drawer flex: 0 0 auto is set
- Confirmation that diagnostic console.log lines removed
notes:
Removed drawer diagnostic logs; opted .bottom-drawer out of .ide-main flex:1.

- forge-3r1   P4  DEFERRED: ContributionHeatmap in sidebar

- forge-ftj: bd show + compare scope against closed forge-kz7. The closed bead derived blocked/ready from dep graph in IssueFeed; if ftj is the same scope, close as duplicate. Memory ack: 'nothing novel'.
- Expected outcome: ~11 active beads.

STEP 1 ŌĆö Two parallel tracks (disjoint file scopes; safe to run concurrently)

TRACK A: observability/deploy
  Order  Bead          Why this slot                                              Estimate
  A.1    forge-a72     Highest leverage ŌĆö unblocks sp merge gate for every       half day
                       future chain. We manually merged 7 commits this session

  A.2a   forge-zpw8    Backend logger; touches src/core/*, src/api/ws/*,         1 day

                       lands clean.

                       wait window in either track.

PARALLEL DISPATCH RULE
- bd commit-gate is project-wide. Parallel executors are fine; the SECOND commit waits until the first chain closes + commits. Plan accordingly: close one chain's bead before starting another's commit.

STEP 2 ŌĆö Console mini-epic (next session+1)

- RECOMMENDATION: promote to an epic with these two as children; consider adding 1-2 more (e.g. live realtime metrics graph backed by forge-zpw8's log stream) for a complete surface.

2. Claim forge-a72 + forge-8kdc; dispatch both executors with --worktree --keep-alive --background.

title: BeadsRepoView: coalesce WS-driven refetch of /issues, /memories, /interactions (forge-h830 follow-up)
description:
## PROBLEM
forge-h830 reduced the beads-change-watcher event storm and added refetch coalescing to useGraphData + useChains. But \`apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/components/beads/BeadsRepoView.tsx\` also subscribes to \`beads:changes\` and triggers refetches for /issues, /memories, /interactions per event. On the running VPS, hot project (market-data) showed **368 hits in 30s each (12/sec)** on those three endpoints ŌĆö that's the residual UI thrash even with the server-side reduction.

## SCOPE
- Replace direct refetch-on-event with the same coalesced setTimeout pattern used in useGraphData / useChains (REFETCH_COALESCE_MS = 1_500).
- Optional: extract \`useCoalescedRefetch(channel, refetch)\` helper into \`apps/gitboard/src/dashboard/hooks/\` so the pattern doesn't drift across three hooks.

## OUTPUT
- Feed view stops thrashing under WS burst conditions.
- /memories /interactions /issues hit rate on the running VPS drops to Ōēż1 hit per 1.5s per active project.
notes:

title: beads-change-watcher: fast-path-breaking lastCommitHash.delete causes snapshot re-read every 2s per project
description:
## PROBLEM
In \`apps/beadboard/src/core/beads-change-watcher.ts\` the \`poll()\` function has a clear bug at L89/L96:

\`\`\`ts
if (commitHash) this.lastCommitHash.set(project.id, commitHash);  // L89
...
const priorHealthy = this.lastHealth.get(project.id);
if (priorHealthy !== healthy) {
  this.lastHealth.set(project.id, healthy);
  emit(...source_health.changed...);
}
else this.lastCommitHash.delete(project.id);                      // L96 ŌĆö DELETES the cache it just set
\`\`\`

For stable, healthy projects (the common case), line 89 sets the commit hash, then line 96's \`else\` immediately deletes it. Next poll cycle's fast-path check at L72 (\`commitHash && prevHash === commitHash && haveSnapshot\`) fails because \`prevHash\` is undefined ŌåÆ full \`readSnapshot()\` fires (1000-row Dolt query + 3 hydration IN-clauses) ŌåÆ \`diffAndQueue\` produces issue.upsert events for every issue (up to 50 batch cap, overflow flush at MAX_BATCH=50).

Net effect on the running VPS: ~60 \`batch.published\` events/second sustained, ~3K WS envelopes/second to subscribers, Dolt circuit breaker tripping on vaultctl, log file 470MB/day.

## CONTEXT
- Comment at L68-71 explicitly states the fast path is the intended optimization. L96 undoes it for the common case.
- Pre-forge-7cyq this was silently broken ŌĆö events were published into channels with no real subscribers thanks to the WS subscribe contract mismatch. Fixing WS exposed the latent storm.

## SCOPE
- Remove the \`else this.lastCommitHash.delete(...)\` branch (or replace with a sensibly-scoped condition if the original intent can be recovered from git history).
- Verify post-fix: \`poll.snapshot_read\` log volume drops from ~6/sec to near-zero for stable projects; \`batch.published\` drops in lockstep; Dolt slow-query / circuit-breaker tripping subsides.
- File a separate follow-up for client-side refetch coalescing in useGraphData / useChains ŌĆö events firing every 2s on a stable project still cause unnecessary refetches even after the count drops.

## OUTPUT
- Sustained log volume on a stable VPS drops by ~95%.
- Dolt server load on the shared 3308 instance drops accordingly.
close_reason:
Server: (a) removed the lastCommitHash.delete that was defeating the fast-path optimization for stable projects, (b) breaker-check + finally-disconnect in getCommitHash so we stop creating leaked DoltClients during Dolt slowdowns, (c) bumped ACTIVE_POLL_MS 2sŌåÆ30s + IDLE_POLL_MS 10sŌåÆ60s ŌĆö fs.watch on each issues.jsonl already provides the real-time signal; the interval is just a backup heartbeat.
Client: useGraphData and useChains now coalesce burst WS events into a single trailing refetch (REFETCH_COALESCE_MS=1500).
Result on VPS: batch.published 51/sec ŌåÆ 8/sec (6├Ś reduction); poll.snapshot_read 6/sec ŌåÆ 0.77/sec (8├Ś reduction); Dolt slow-query / breaker activity dropped accordingly.
Residual thrash localised to BeadsRepoView which has its own non-coalesced refetch ŌĆö separate follow-up filed.

title: Codex regressions on PRs #31/#32: dedupe depth-0 scan, restore include_closed, add in_review
description:
## PROBLEM
Three legitimate Codex review findings on the already-merged PRs:

1. **P1 ŌĆö apps/beadboard/src/core/project-scanner.ts** (PR #31): the depth-0 fall-through introduced in forge-qa5g pushes `loaded` projects then unconditionally recurses, so flat layouts (`<root>/<repo>/.beads`) are discovered twice ŌĆö once via the fast path, once via recursion. Causes duplicate rows in /api/beads/projects (already visible in earlier output: \"barchart-scraper\", \"sierra-vps\", \"second-mind\" all appeared twice).

2. **P1 ŌĆö apps/gitboard/src/core/graph-dao.ts:202** (PR #32): the hardcoded status filter `[open, in_progress, blocked, deferred]` means Dolt-backed graph reads never return closed rows. `/api/console/graph?include_closed=true` silently behaves like `include_closed=false` for those projects ŌĆö endpoint contract regressed.

3. **P2 ŌĆö apps/gitboard/src/core/graph-dao.ts:202** (PR #32): same filter drops `in_review` status, which is a valid Beads status normalised in `normalizeStatus`. Projects with `in_review` issues silently lose them from graph nodes/edges.

## SCOPE
- project-scanner.ts: track names loaded at depth 0; skip them in the subsequent recursion loop (no duplicate). Preserve nested-layout discovery.
- graph-dao.ts readIssues: parameterise by `includeClosed`; pass through from `getGraphSnapshot`. Status-filter at SQL only when `includeClosed=false`. Cache key gains the includeClosed bit.
- graph-dao.ts status filter: add `in_review` to the non-closed set.

## OUTPUT
- /api/beads/projects shows each repo exactly once for any layout (flat or nested).
- /api/console/graph?include_closed=true once again returns closed nodes for Dolt-backed projects.
- in_review issues appear in graph nodes & edges.
close_reason:
Fixed three Codex findings on merged PRs:
1. project-scanner depth-0 dedup: track flatLoadedChildren in a Set, skip those names in the recursive for-loop. Result: 25 ŌåÆ 21 projects (no spurious dups). Remaining same-name entries are real distinct repos at different paths.
2. graph-dao readIssues + issueCacheKey now parameterised by includeClosed. When true, query is unfiltered (limit 2000) and the returned 1945 closed + 55 active rows confirm the endpoint contract is restored.
3. ACTIVE_GRAPH_STATUSES constant now includes 'in_review' alongside open/in_progress/blocked/deferred. normalizeStatus already mapped in_review ŌåÆ in_progress in buildGraph; the SQL filter is now consistent with that allowlist.

title: Live specialist updates via WS push (specialists:activity channel)
description:
## PROBLEM
Specialists/observability state is the dashboard's primary live data, but unlike beads (BeadsChangeWatcher ŌåÆ "beads:changes" ŌåÆ useWebSocket ŌåÆ in-place patch) and GitHub (poller ŌåÆ "github:*" ŌåÆ WS), the specialist side has no WS pipeline at all. The observability watcher detects sqlite mtime changes and calls `epoch.bump(repoSlug)` (`apps/gitboard/src/server/observability/epoch.ts`), but no one consumes that signal to publish ŌĆö the counter is read on-demand by REST routes only.

Consequence: graph specialist overlay and the specialists drawer go stale silently between page interactions. The graph specialist overlay was the main contributor to the ~300ms warm-graph latency (forge-ao1g) because `dao.inFlightJobs()` is called per request. forge-ao1g proposed a TTL cache as remediation; pushing instead removes the need for that cache entirely.

## SCOPE ŌĆö Phase 1
- **Server**: tiny listener registry in `epoch.ts` (`onBump(cb): () => void`). On startup, `api/server.ts` registers one listener that calls `registry.publish("specialists:activity", "specialists:sync_hint", { repoSlug }, epochValue)`. Mirrors the existing `BeadsChangeWatcher` pattern exactly.
- **Channel/event types**: add `"specialists:activity"` to `ChannelName` and `"specialists:sync_hint"` to the realtime event union.
- **Dashboard**: `useGraphData` adds a second `useWebSocket("specialists:activity", ...)` that triggers refetch. `useChains` gains its first `useWebSocket("specialists:activity", ...)` + refetch.
- Reuse existing helpers; do not invent new abstractions. Helpers welcome for *reusable* patterns (e.g. a `useRefetchOnSyncHint(channel, refetch)` thin wrapper if both consumers want the exact same behaviour).

## NON_GOALS (defer to Phase 2)
- Per-repo channels (`specialists:<repoSlug>`) ŌĆö Phase 1 broadcasts a single hint per repo bump, client refetches if subscribed.
- Diff/batched payloads (specialists:batch with full job rows) ŌĆö Phase 1 is hint-only; the client refetches the cached REST endpoint.
- Multi-tab dedup.

## OUTPUT
- Graph specialist overlay updates push-driven (Ōēż400ms after disk write).
- Specialists drawer updates push-driven.
- forge-ao1g's per-call inFlightJobs SQL cost becomes irrelevant (no longer hit every render).
close_reason:
Phase 1 shipped. Server side: epoch.ts exposes onBump listener registry; api/server.ts registers one listener at createApp() that publishes 'specialists:sync_hint' on the new 'specialists:activity' channel; ws/handler.ts replay-gap path picks the right sync_hint event-name when channel.startsWith('specialists:'). Client side: useGraphData adds a 2nd useWebSocket on specialists:activity that invalidates the local graph cache + force-refetches; useChains gains its first useWebSocket subscription that clears its POLL_MS timer and reloads on hint.

Critical bonus fix: discovered while smoke-testing that WsClient.subscribe sent {type:'subscribe'} while the server handler routes on msg.action ŌĆö a long-standing mismatch that silently dropped every WS subscription. Fixed to {action:'subscribe'}, which unlocks live updates for ALL channels (beads:changes, github:*, specialists:activity, system). E2E smoke test confirms: a single observability db mtime change now delivers a sync_hint to a subscribed client in <800ms (200ms watcher debounce + publish + WS roundtrip). beads:changes flowed 530 events in 7s vs 0 before the fix.

Validated by overthinker (4-phase devil's advocate review) and code-sanity (READ_ONLY smell pass) specialists. Addressed findings: replaced silent listener catch with console.error; consolidated cleanup pattern with existing currentObservabilityWatcher disposal. Deferred to Phase 2 / separate beads: per-repo channels for finer filtering (refetch-storm hardening), lifecycle test for createApp re-entry, typed boundary helper for beads:changes union payloads.

Supersedes forge-ao1g (the per-call inFlightJobs SQL cost is no longer the hot path because the graph endpoint isn't hit on every render ŌĆö it's hit on push events).

title: beadboard scanner: missing metadata.dolt_database fallback (port from gitboard scanner)
description:
## PROBLEM
forge-qa5g ported the shared-server port fallback from gitboard's project-scanner.ts into beadboard's, but missed the *second* line gitboard's scanner has right next to it:

    if (!doltDatabase && metadata.dolt_database) doltDatabase = metadata.dolt_database;

Without this, projects like specialists ŌĆö whose .beads/config.yaml does NOT set `dolt_database` but whose .beads/metadata.json does (`"dolt_database": "unitAI"`) ŌĆö get queried against the shared Dolt server with no database name set. The client falls back to "dolt", which is either empty or a different project. Symptom: specialists shows 65 open issues via `bd list` but 7 nodes in the dashboard graph / wrong counts in sidebar; same likely applies to every project that relies on metadata.json for the dolt_database.

## SCOPE
- Mirror gitboard scanner line 139 in beadboard scanner loadProject().
- Verify all 18 projects with `doltPort: 3308` resolve to the correct DB.

## OUTPUT
- Patched beadboard scanner
- Spot-check: specialists graph + sidebar count matches bd list
close_reason: Fixed two bugs: (1) beadboard scanner now reads dolt_database from metadata.json fallback (mirrors gitboard scanner line 139) ŌĆö specialists now resolves to db 'unitAI' instead of falling back to 'dolt'; (2) graph-dao readIssues now filters status at SQL (status IN open/in_progress/blocked/deferred) with limit 2000 ŌĆö projects with thousands of closed issues no longer silently truncate open ones. Specialists graph went from 7 nodes to 83 (62 open + 19 deferred + 2 blocked), matching CLI counts. Follow-up forge-XXXX filed for inFlightJobs() per-call SQL perf.

title: Specialists dao sync queries starve event loop in background ŌĆö third forge-5i43 sibling
description:
## PROBLEM
After forge-ijzx fixed cold-parallel head-of-line blocking, a secondary pattern remains:

Live measurement (2026-05-21, post forge-ijzx merge):
- Cold parallel graph + specialists + beads: 13/25/27ms (FAST ŌĆö fixed)
- Warm parallel graph + specialists 500ms later: graph 143ms / **specialists 1614ms**

Root cause: /api/specialists/jobs/* returns immediately with stale-empty (good), then fires `void refreshInFlight(dao, ...)` in the background. refreshInFlight calls `dao.inFlightJobs()` and `dao.recentJobs(limit)` SYNCHRONOUSLY ŌĆö these execute SQLite queries across the attached pool (~30 dbs) on the Bun event loop. Each query yields nothing until done; the cumulative work is ~1.5s of sync sqlite work.

When wave 2 fires while this background work is still running, the new specialists request lands behind the in-progress sync sqlite calls and waits ~1.5s for the event loop to free up.

Pattern: same family as forge-9qmk (listRepos sync FS) and forge-ijzx (createAttachPool sync ATTACH) ŌĆö sync work in 'background' that still blocks the event loop. Memory key: when-writing-bead-ac-for-performance-ha-work.

## SUCCESS
- Warm parallel graph + specialists: BOTH responses <300ms regardless of timing relative to a previous specialists refresh.
- dao.inFlightJobs() and dao.recentJobs() either:
  (a) yield to the event loop between attached-repo iterations, OR
  (b) the BACKGROUND refresh splits work across event-loop ticks.
- No regression in returned data shape.

## SCOPE
- apps/gitboard/src/server/observability/dao.ts (createObservabilityDao + the per-repo query loop inside inFlightJobs/recentJobs/jobsByBead/chainById ŌĆö wherever it iterates over attached repos).
- apps/gitboard/src/api/routes/specialists.ts (refreshInFlight ŌĆö batch the dao calls if dao itself can't yield).
- Integration test: fire warm specialists request, wait 100ms (during background refresh), fire concurrent graph request, assert graph <300ms.

## NON_GOALS
- Re-architecting the observability schema.
- Changing dao return shape.

## CONSTRAINTS
- Preserve query semantics (same rows returned).
- The background refresh can still take total ~1.5s wall-clock ŌĆö but must chunk across ticks so other routes can interleave.

## FAIL gate
Reviewer must verify with a CONCURRENT integration test (one slow request in flight while another fast request fires). A solo-request unit test does NOT prove the fix. Cite the bead's memory key when reviewing.

## VALIDATION
- Integration test: trigger refreshInFlight; while it's mid-flight, fire /api/console/graph; assert <300ms.
- Manual probe: bun script firing specialists then graph 500ms later ŌĆö graph should be <300ms.

## OUTPUT
Changed files, integration test evidence, residual risks.

title: createAttachPool blocks event loop ~1.5s on first parallel request ŌĆö forge-5i43 spirit fix
description:
## PROBLEM
forge-5i43 made attach-pool init 'async' by wrapping it in Promise.resolve().then(() => createAttachPool(repos)). The microtask deferral satisfied the bead's concurrent-cold-init unit test (which only tested SOLO request behavior), but in PRACTICE createAttachPool still opens all ~30 SQLite ATTACHes synchronously in one tick, which holds the Bun event loop for 1.5s. Any concurrent request ŌĆö including the graph route the user is trying to load ŌĆö is blocked until ATTACH completes.

Measured live in dev (2026-05-21):
- 3x graph parallel = 141/276/422 ms (no contention)
- graph + specialists parallel = graph 1662ms, specialists 1530ms (specialists' attach-pool warm blocks graph)

User symptom: 'graph takes forever to load' when navigating to /console for the first time, even after forge-5i43 + forge-9qmk merged.

This is the SECOND time forge-5i43's AC was met by name but missed in spirit (first was listRepos in forge-9qmk). Pattern: 'sync work moved to a microtask is still sync work'.

## SUCCESS
- Single concurrent request that hits an unwarmed attach-pool MUST NOT block unrelated routes for more than ~50ms total.
- graph + specialists parallel from cold state: graph response <300ms (specialists may still be stale-empty on first call, that is fine).
- Repeated parallel hits stay flat (no degradation).

## SCOPE
- apps/gitboard/src/server/observability/attach-pool.ts ŌĆö createAttachPool needs to YIELD between repos. Options (pick simplest that works):
  (a) Convert to async and await each ATTACH with setImmediate/queueMicrotask between batches of ~5.
  (b) Lazy-attach: open ATTACH on first dao query needing it, not eagerly.
  (c) Move to worker_thread.
- apps/gitboard/src/api/routes/specialists.ts ŌĆö getDefaultBundle/warmDefaultBundle: if (a) or (c) is chosen, adapt the warm contract.
- Test: write an INTEGRATION test that fires graph + specialists in parallel from cold state and asserts graph response <300ms. THIS is the gate the prior bead's unit test missed.

## NON_GOALS
- Re-architecting observability dao.
- Changing the freshness contract.

## CONSTRAINTS
- Preserve current return shapes.
- Preserve listRepos cache (forge-9qmk).
- The cold attach-pool initialization can still take 1.5s wall-clock TOTAL ŌĆö but it must do so off the request path, chunked across event-loop ticks.

## FAIL gate
A unit test that returns synchronously fast WHEN NO OTHER REQUEST IS COMPETING does NOT prove this fix. description:

forge-5i43 made Dolt ATTACH async but missed a sibling sync FS scan: `listRepos()` in apps/gitboard/src/server/observability/registry.ts walks the filesystem on every call (readdirSync/statSync across all observability roots, ~30 project dirs).

Discovered while testing forge-5i43 in dev ŌĆö user reported slow specialists tab.

## SUCCESS

- Cache invalidated by file-watcher signal OR a small refresh interval (5-10s). NOT a per-request scan.
- /api/specialists/jobs/in-flight p95 drops from ~1.5s to <50ms on cache hit.
- No behavior change in cache miss (first request still scans).

- Fixing the schema_version 0 incompatible observability DB warning (separate bead).
- Fixing graph project UUID resolution (separate bead).

## CONSTRAINTS

- Cache invalidation must not require restart in dev; a 5-10s refresh window is acceptable.
- Pattern should mirror the (key, refreshAt) shape already used in graph-dao / specialists.

## VALIDATION

- Unit test: advance fake timers past refresh window; next call re-scans.
- Manual probe: curl /api/specialists/jobs/in-flight twice; second call <100ms.

Changed files, test evidence, residual risks.

title: Research dashboard data-plane HA patterns for realtime cached reads
description:
## PROBLEM

Gitboard needs high-availability dashboard reads: local cache/DB should always serve visible data fast, WebSocket remains freshness authority, expensive GitHub REST/specialist scans run off the request path. Need current external evidence on whether to use in-process cache, SQLite read models/materialized views, background workers, Redis/Valkey, or queue/event bus patterns for a single-node/container dashboard.

## SUCCESS

Produce recommendation for this repo: what technology/pattern to use now, when Redis is justified, and how to structure ingestion vs serving for GitHub and specialists without overengineering.

## SCOPE

Research current practices for:
- stale-while-revalidate API serving from local DB/cache
- background ingestion workers and read-model tables
- WebSocket merge/freshness semantics
- SQLite WAL/concurrency in Bun/Node dashboards
- Redis/Valkey tradeoffs for single-node vs multi-process containers

## NON_GOALS

No code edits. Do not map local code deeply; focus on external/current technology guidance.

## OUTPUT

Short findings, recommended architecture, Redis yes/no decision criteria, concrete implementation pattern for Gitboard.
notes:
Started research: current external evidence for HA dashboard reads, SWR, SQLite WAL, Redis/Valkey tradeoffs.
### Specialist Output ŌĆö researcher (job cecd9d) [WAITING ŌĆö more output may follow]

## Summary
- **Gitboard now**: `SQLite WAL` + **background ingestor** + **local read-model tables** + **WebSocket delta/freshness signal**. Fast reads from local DB. Freshness from ws. Heavy GitHub/specialist work off request path.
- **Redis/Valkey now**: **no**, unless need cross-process fan-out, durable queue, or multi-node scale.
- **Why**: SQLite WAL gives concurrent reads with one writer; SWR cache pattern fits dashboard snapshots; Redis adds ops/cross-process power you do not need yet.

## Status
- Research done.
- No code edits.

## Changes
- None.

## Findings
- **SWR cache pattern current**: `stale-while-revalidate` serves stale response immediately, revalidates in background. Good fit for dashboard snapshot endpoints.  
  Source: MDN Cache-Control docs ŌĆö https://github.com/mdn/content/blob/main/files/en-us/web/http/reference/headers/cache-control/index.md
- **SQLite WAL current**: readers and writers can run same time, but **only one writer at time**. Checkpoints can run with readers, but stop for active readers.  
  Source: SQLite WAL docs ŌĆö https://www.sqlite.org/wal.html
- **Bun/SQLite current**: Bun ships dashboard-style SQLite server example; Bun source shows shared sqlite handle across workers and explicit ŌĆ£racy if using WorkerŌĆØ comment, so keep writes serialized.  
  Sources: https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/docs/bundler/fullstack.mdx  
  https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/blob/main/bun/src/jsc/bindings/sqlite/JSSQLStatement.cpp
- **Redis current**: Redis is cache + message broker + stream engine. Client-side caching reduces repeated requests, latency, DB load. Streams consumer groups (`XREADGROUP`) give queue semantics with ack/pending state.  
  Sources: https://redis.io/docs/latest/operate/rs/databases/memory-performance  
  https://redis.io/docs/latest/commands/xreadgroup  
  https://redis.io/docs/get-started

## Recommended architecture
- **Serve path**
  - Read only from local SQLite.
  - Tables: `projects`, `issues`, `signals`, `sync_state`, maybe denormalized `dashboard_*` read models.
  - Endpoints return from DB only. No GitHub REST on request path.
- **Ingest path**
  - One worker/job loop pulls GitHub REST + specialist scan results.
  - Worker writes normalized facts, then updates read-model tables in same DB transaction.
  - Worker bumps monotonic `seq` / `updated_at` / version token.
- **Freshness path**
  - WebSocket broadcasts `{entity, seq, changed_ids}`.
  - Client merges if seq fits; else refetch snapshot.
  - If gap / restart / unknown seq, force full resync.
- **Queue pattern**
  - Start with in-process job queue or SQLite-backed job table.
  - Add Redis Streams only if worker pool must survive process split or multiple containers.

## Redis yes/no
- **No Redis** when:
  - single node/container
  - one SQLite file on local disk
  - one writer worker
  - ws only for freshness
  - tolerate short staleness
- **Yes Redis** when:
  - multiple app containers need shared queue/fan-out
  - background jobs must survive app restarts independently
  - multiple producers/consumers across services
  - need pub/sub or streams outside same process
  - need external cache shared by many app instances

## Concrete Gitboard pattern
- **Read model**: SQLite WAL tables.
- **Ingestion**: background worker.
- **Serving**: snapshot reads from DB, optional HTTP `ETag`/version.
- **WebSocket**: invalidate / delta only.
- **GitHub/specialists**: async ingest, never request path.
- **Redis**: defer until multi-process boundary.

## Risks
- Hot write rate can hit SQLite single-writer ceiling.
- If ws sequence gaps not handled, client can drift stale.
- If app splits into many containers, local-only queue stops scaling.

## Follow-ups
- If want, I can turn this into exact Gitboard component split: tables, worker loop, ws message shape, retry rules.

## Beads
- Claimed `forge-o9iw`.
- Closed `forge-o9iw` after research.

```json
{"status":"success","summary":"Research complete: use SQLite WAL read model + background ingestion + ws freshness; skip Redis until cross-process or multi-node need.","files_changed":[],"follow_ups":["Map pattern onto Gitboard tables, worker loop, ws message shape if needed."],"risks":["SQLite single-writer ceiling if write volume climbs.","Redis adds ops/cross-process cost before needed."],"verification":["Checked SQLite WAL docs, MDN Cache-Control stale-while-revalidate, Bun SQLite docs/source, Redis client-side caching and Streams docs."]}
```

title: Fix Codex follow-ups from realtime dashboard epic PRs
description:
## PROBLEM

Codex review comments on merged forge-ojn4 PRs #22, #23, and #24 identified follow-up regressions or weak guards that were not addressed before merge. These affect GitHub poll freshness under failures, Retry-After backoff monotonicity, specialists router option typing/runtime safety, and backend boundary test strength.

## SUCCESS

All Codex findings are addressed with focused code/test changes: failed GitHub polls do not advance `last_polled_at`, concurrent rate-limit responses cannot shrink a longer pause, `createSpecialistsRouter` default bundle path requires/handles full repo metadata, and boundary contract tests assert runnable test declarations rather than raw text matches.

## SCOPE

- `apps/gitboard/src/core/github-poller.ts`
- `apps/gitboard/tests/core/github-poller-loop.test.ts`
- `apps/gitboard/src/api/routes/specialists.ts`
- `apps/gitboard/tests/api/routes/specialists.test.ts`
- `apps/gitboard/tests/backend-boundaries/performance-boundary-contract.test.ts`

## NON_GOALS

- No broader GitHub poller redesign.
- No new endpoint cache strategy.
- No changes to merged forge-ojn4 feature behavior beyond Codex findings.
- No full-suite cleanup for unrelated existing failures.

## CONSTRAINTS

- Preserve instant WebSocket behavior and existing response shapes.
- Keep fixes minimal and compatible with existing tests.
- Run GitNexus impact before editing modified runtime symbols.
- Validate with targeted tests for poller, specialists router, and boundary contract.

## VALIDATION

- [ ] `bun --filter @xtrm/gitboard test -- tests/core/github-poller-loop.test.ts tests/api/routes/specialists.test.ts tests/backend-boundaries/performance-boundary-contract.test.ts`
- [ ] Code-sanity pass or explicit targeted review.
- [ ] `gitnexus_detect_changes` before commit.

## OUTPUT

title: Critique Dolt runtime isolation plan
description: PROBLEM: forge-ojn4.2 changes critical Dolt/mysql2 connection lifecycle behavior. A design mistake could make project switching slower, leak pools/timers, or hide degraded-source fallback regressions. SUCCESS: provide a concise go/no-go critique of the implementation plan before executor edits: runtime key shape, pool lifecycle, breaker/probe isolation, fallback behavior, and tests. SCOPE: apps/beadboard/src/core/dolt-client.ts, apps/beadboard/src/api/routes/beads.ts, relevant tests and forge-ojn4.2 research notes. NON_GOALS: no code edits, no broad storage rewrite, no new external dependencies unless strongly justified. CONSTRAINTS: READ_ONLY; preserve existing DoltClient public methods and JSONL/SQLite fallback; WebSocket/dashboard realtime must not regress. VALIDATION: findings cite files/symbols or research notes and name concrete acceptance checks for executor. OUTPUT: PASS/CONCERNS with recommended runtime model, must-have tests, and any red flags.
notes:
### Specialist Output ŌĆö overthinker (job 083f75) [WAITING ŌĆö more output may follow]

## Summary

CONCERNS. Current isolation model must change before executor edits merge.

Core risk: singleton `runtime` in `apps/beadboard/src/core/dolt-client.ts` makes all `DoltClient` instances share pool, breaker, probe timer. Project switch can close other project pool, leak behavior across realtime watcher, hide fallback regressions.

## Phase 1 - Initial Analysis

Goal: isolate Dolt/mysql2 runtime per connection identity.

Evidence:
- `DoltClient.connected` checks singleton `runtime.poolKey` against `poolKey(this.config)`.
- `ensurePool` ends existing `runtime.pool` when key differs.
- `recordFailure`, `execute`, `probeHealth`, `runProbe` use shared `runtime.breakerOpenUntil` and `runtime.consecutiveFailures`.
- `getDoltClient` in `apps/beadboard/src/api/routes/beads.ts` caches by `port` only.
- `BeadsChangeWatcher.readIssues` and `getCommitHash` create new `DoltClient` per project, so singleton runtime affects WebSocket/dashboard realtime paths.

Recommended runtime key:
`host + port + database + user` minimum. Include future auth/socket fields if added. Do not log secret. Route cache must use same key, not `port` only.

Recommended runtime model:
- `Map<runtimeKey, DoltRuntime>`
- each runtime owns `pool`, `breaker`, `probeTimer`, `probing`, failure counters, metrics
- `DoltClient` stores key, resolves runtime by key
- `disconnect()` closes only own runtime
- probe timer per runtime, `unref()`, cleared on runtime close
- no global pool close on project switch

## Phase 2 - Devil's Advocate

Singleton pool looks simpler, but breaks isolation:
- Project A query creates pool A.
- Project B query calls `ensurePool`, sees different key, calls `runtime.pool.end()`.
- Project A next query reconnects. Switching gets slower.
- Failure in project A opens breaker for project B because breaker state shared.
- Probe timer created by project A can run through instance method with project A config while runtime now points at project B pool.
- `runProbe` calls `execute`, and `execute` respects breaker. During open breaker, recovery probe cannot test until breaker expires. Acceptable only if half-open request path exists and tested.
- Route fallback catches Dolt errors and continues, but too much catch can hide regression unless tests assert degraded behavior and source preference.
- `getDoltClient(port)` ignores database. Same port + different Dolt database aliases same client. Bad key.

## Phase 3 - Synthesis

Go only if executor changes plan to per-key runtime map. Current singleton design: NO-GO.

Keep public API stable:
- preserve `connect`, `disconnect`, `isConnected`, `isBreakerOpen`, `getIssues`, `getIssue`, `getClosedIssues`, `getCommitHash`, `fetchIssuesSince`
- preserve SQLite/JSONL fallback in `apps/beadboard/src/api/routes/beads.ts`
- preserve realtime watcher behavior in `apps/beadboard/src/core/beads-change-watcher.ts`

Must-have tests:
1. Two clients with different ports keep separate pools; client B does not end client A pool.
2. Two clients same port different database get different runtime keys.
3. Failure opens breaker for runtime A only; runtime B still queries.
4. `disconnect()` closes only caller runtime pool/timer; other runtime remains connected.
5. Fake timers prove probe timers clear on disconnect and use `unref()`.
6. Route Dolt failure falls back to SQLite, then JSONL.
7. Breaker-open Dolt path falls back fast without blocking dashboard.
8. Realtime watcher commit hash/readIssues across two Dolt projects does not churn pools or share breaker.

Acceptance checks:
- `gitnexus_detect_changes` currently reports high risk in `apps/beadboard/src/core/dolt-client.ts`; executor must limit final touched scope to Dolt client + route tests.
- No new external dependency.
- No broad storage rewrite.
- Dashboard/WebSocket realtime still receives bead updates when one project Dolt is down and another is healthy.

## Phase 4 - Final Refined Output

Recommendation: CONCERNS / NO-GO for singleton runtime. GO only after per-runtime-key map model accepted.

Red flags:
- `poolKey` good start but runtime still singleton.
- `getDoltClient(port)` cache key too weak.
- shared breaker/probe state violates project isolation.
- `ensurePool` closes global pool on key change, causing switch churn.
- fallback paths silent; must test degraded-source behavior.

## Verification

Used GitNexus:
- `DoltClient`
- `getDoltClient`
- `getIssuesFromProject`
- `getIssueDetailFromProject`
- `getClosedIssuesFromProject`
- `ensurePool`
- `execute`
- `probeHealth`
- `runProbe`
- `recordFailure`
- `BeadsChangeWatcher.readIssues`
- `BeadsChangeWatcher.getCommitHash`
- `gitnexus_detect_changes`

No files edited.

## Beads

Could not run `bd update` / `bd close`: no shell tool available in specialist harness.

## Final Answer

CONCERNS. Do not proceed with singleton `runtime`. Executor should implement per-key runtime map, route cache by full runtime key, isolated pools, isolated breaker/probe state, targeted fallback tests. Then GO.

  ],
